title: Phone-footage meet-cute with preserved dialogue
brief:
A realistic phone-footage style scene: two strangers accidentally swap identical tote bags at a rainy bus stop. They realize it immediately and laugh. Keep it grounded and causal, with a few clear beats. Preserve the dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
ALEX: "Wait—this is yours. We have the same bag."
MAYA: "No way. Okay, you’re definitely getting the one with the leaky umbrella."

title: Single-subject continuity test with prop
brief: A single subject (an older man with a distinctive red knit beanie) walks through a busy flea market. He is holding a small vintage camera with a leather strap. He stops, takes one photo, smiles, and continues walking. No dialogue.

title: Two-person dialogue attribution stress test
brief:
Two friends sit at a kitchen table. One (Jules) is chopping vegetables; the other (Rina) is pouring tea. Keep the framing cinematic medium/medium-close during dialogue and avoid fast cuts. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
JULES: "If I chop any slower, we’ll eat tomorrow."
RINA: "Good. Tomorrow me is hungrier."

title: Two-person dialogue with similar silhouettes (coat + hat)
brief:
Realistic phone-footage look, but use cinematic coverage during dialogue (medium and medium-close), not selfie. Two coworkers stand under an awning outside a cafe during light rain. Both wear dark coats; one wears a black beanie, the other a hood. Keep only one mouth clearly visible while each line is spoken. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
SAM: "I swear the rain follows me." 
TAYLOR: "Maybe it just likes your dramatic entrances."

title: Over-the-shoulder coverage to prevent mouth swapping
brief:
Two friends in a small bookstore aisle. Use classic shot-reverse-shot coverage: over-the-shoulder medium shots and occasional medium-close on the current speaker. Avoid cutting mid-line. Keep the background consistent (same shelf labels, same lamp). Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
NORA: "You keep buying cookbooks like they’re going to cook for you." 
ELI: "They inspire the version of me who has free time."

title: Occlusion stress test (one face partly obscured)
brief:
Two siblings in a kitchen at night. One (Casey) is facing camera in medium-close; the other (Morgan) is in profile and sometimes partially occluded by a cabinet door. Stage dialogue so only the speaking mouth is clearly visible. Avoid any cutaways during spoken words. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
CASEY: "Did you move my mug again?" 
MORGAN: "No. The mug moved itself. It’s evolving."

title: Both faces in frame (high-risk) but enforce turn-taking
brief:
Two roommates sit on a couch, both faces visible in a single medium two-shot. This is a stress test: keep turn-taking crystal clear so each line is attributed to the correct mouth. No cuts during lines. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
LEO: "If we clean for ten minutes, we can be messy guilt-free." 
PRIYA: "That’s not how guilt works, but I admire your optimism."

title: Crossing movement between speakers (blocking test)
brief:
Two people (Ari and Wen) stand at a whiteboard in an office. Wen steps forward to point at the board, briefly crossing in front of Ari. Keep dialogue staged so the current speaker’s mouth is visible; if someone crosses, pause speech until visibility returns. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
ARI: "So the plan is: we pretend this chart makes sense." 
WEN: "And then we confidently nod until someone else speaks first."

title: Off-screen speaker trap (avoid it)
brief:
Two friends at a street food stand at dusk. Keep both speakers on-screen when they speak; do not have an off-screen voice. Use medium/medium-close coverage and avoid cutting away to the food during lines. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
MILA: "Tell me you didn’t order the spiciest one." 
DEV: "I ordered the spiciest one. For character development."

title: Whisper/low amplitude dialogue (lip-sync plausibility)
brief:
Two friends in a quiet library corner. Dialogue is spoken softly; lip movements should be subtle and plausible. Use medium-close on the current speaker. No cuts during lines. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
KIM: "If we get kicked out, it’s on you." 
OWEN: "I’m whispering. This is my quietest trouble."

title: Laughing overlap risk (avoid simultaneous speech)
brief:
Two friends walk under string lights in a backyard. One tells a joke; the other laughs after the line ends. Do not overlap speech; keep one speaker at a time. Use phone-footage feel but cinematic medium/medium-close during lines. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
HARPER: "I tried jogging once. My body filed a complaint." 
ROWAN: "Valid. Your body knows its rights."

title: Matching hair + glasses (identity + attribution)
brief:
Two colleagues (Jae and Luca) sit side-by-side at a small conference table. Both have short dark hair and wear glasses (stress test). Use shot design to make attribution unambiguous: when Jae speaks, frame Jae in medium-close while Luca is out of focus or off-frame; then switch. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
JAE: "We could label it 'draft' and hope no one reads it." 
LUCA: "Or label it 'final' and guarantee everyone reads it."

title: Hands/prop continuity during dialogue (mug)
brief:
Two friends at a dining table. One (Sasha) has a bright yellow mug; the other (Ben) has a plain white mug. Keep mugs consistent in hands across the entire scene. Stage dialogue with medium-close on the current speaker and avoid cutting mid-line. Preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
SASHA: "If you drink my tea, you inherit my problems." 
BEN: "Tempting. I could use new problems."

title: Mirror/reflection confusion test
brief:
Two people talk in a bathroom while getting ready for an event. There is a mirror in frame (stress test). Ensure the correct person’s mouth is speaking, not the reflection or swapped identity. Use medium/medium-close, avoid fast cuts, preserve dialogue exactly.

Dialogue:
AVA: "I can’t tell if this is 'polished' or 'trying too hard.'" 
NOAH: "It’s polished. 'Trying too hard' would include glitter."

title: Single-subject continuity with costume detail (non-dialogue)
brief: Single subject continuity test: a young woman wearing a distinctive green scarf with a white stripe walks through a train station, checks a paper ticket, and boards a train. Keep scarf and ticket consistent across cuts. No dialogue.
